weak_answer.requirements: Users send a prompt and get a completion back. We should support multiple models.
weak_answer.core_entities: A request has a prompt and a model name. A server holds the model in memory.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: A load balancer sends requests to a pool of servers, each running a copy of the model. Each server processes one request at a time.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd add more servers if it gets slow.

strong_answer.requirements: Stream completions token-by-token, support fast/cheap and slow/high-quality model variants behind one API, and separate interactive (low TTFT, target ~200ms P50) from batch (throughput-optimized) traffic so a long batch job never starves interactive latency.
strong_answer.core_entities: Request (prompt, model, priority tier), Sequence (a request's in-flight generation state plus the KV cache blocks it owns), KV cache block (fixed-size chunk of GPU memory, owned by one sequence or shared via prefix caching), Model replica (loaded weights, tensor-parallel shards).
strong_answer.api_interface: POST /v1/completions { model, prompt, max_tokens, stream, priority } -> stream of { token, finish_reason }.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: A router classifies requests by model and priority, then a scheduler admits sequences into the next forward pass. The single highest-leverage decision is continuous batching -- folding new sequences into the running batch every decode step instead of waiting for a static batch to finish -- which is the difference between 20-30% and 70-90%+ GPU utilization.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    C[Client] --> LB[Router]
    LB --> Q[Priority queue]
    Q --> SCHED[Scheduler]
    SCHED --> ENG1[Replica 1]
    SCHED --> ENG2[Replica 2]
strong_answer.deep_dives: KV cache, not weights, is usually the binding memory constraint at high concurrency -- for a 70B model it can exceed the weights' own footprint at long context. PagedAttention manages this like OS virtual memory: fixed-size blocks allocated on demand, with copy-on-write for shared prefixes, and an explicit preemption policy (pick a victim, swap its KV cache to CPU or recompute later) when memory runs out. Separately, GPU-hours are the dominant cost line, so real per-request token accounting feeding a budget-enforcement gate is a first-class design input, not a monitoring dashboard bolted on after launch -- and the cost/quality trade-off (quantization, speculative decoding, smaller model + more parallelism) needs concrete numbers, not 'it depends.'

weak_answer.requirements: Users ask a question and get an answer with sources. We embed documents and search for similar ones.
weak_answer.core_entities: A document, a chunk, an embedding, a query.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: We chunk documents, embed them into a vector database, and at query time embed the question and find the closest chunks, then pass them to the LLM as context.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd use a bigger vector database if search gets slow.

strong_answer.requirements: Retrieval must respect access control (a user should never see chunks they're not authorized to read), support hybrid lexical + semantic retrieval, and every claim in the generated answer must trace to a specific citation with an explicit grounding check.
strong_answer.core_entities: Chunk (content, content_hash, ingested_at, source_uri, access policy), Embedding, Query, Citation (chunk reference the answer actually used), AccessPolicy (per-user/group read scope).
strong_answer.api_interface: POST /ask { query, principal } -> { answer, citations: [{chunk_id, ...}], grounded: bool }
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: The design decision that matters most: access control is enforced at candidate generation, before ranking -- not as a post-filter. Ranking first and filtering after leaks information through ranking position, and wastes retrieval budget scoring documents that get thrown away anyway.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    Q[Query] --> AUTH[Access policy filter]
    AUTH --> RETRIEVE[Hybrid retrieval: lexical + semantic]
    RETRIEVE --> RANK[Rank authorized candidates]
    RANK --> LLM[Generate + cite]
    LLM --> GUARD[Grounding check]
strong_answer.deep_dives: Hybrid retrieval (BM25 lexical + embedding similarity, combined explicitly) beats either alone -- embeddings miss exact-match/trending signals. Real ingestion lineage (content_hash, ingested_at persisted per chunk) matters because a documented real bug showed object reconstruction during a later pipeline stage can silently drop lineage fields to their defaults, breaking 'what did this answer actually cite' auditability. Grounding needs enforcement at the output stage too: every claim should trace to a specific citation, with an explicit check flagging ungrounded or hallucinated claims rather than assuming retrieval implies grounding.

weak_answer.requirements: The agent should be able to call tools like search and calculator to answer questions.
weak_answer.core_entities: An agent, a tool, a task.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: The agent loops: decide which tool to call, call it, get the result, decide what to do next, until it has an answer.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd add more tools as needed.

strong_answer.requirements: Orchestration (deciding what to do next) must be a separate layer from governance (deciding whether an action is allowed) -- governance can't live inside the agent's own reasoning, because a prompt-injected or simply mistaken agent will confidently request the wrong action. Every tool call needs an allow-list per tool/agent identity, a human-approval gate above a stated risk threshold, and a kill-switch that can halt an in-flight multi-step plan.
strong_answer.core_entities: Agent identity, Tool (with an allow-list scope), Plan (a sequence of intended actions), Governance decision (allow/deny/require-approval per call), Kill-switch state.
strong_answer.api_interface: POST /agent/run { task, agent_identity } -> { plan_id }; every tool call inside the plan routes through POST /governance/authorize { agent_identity, tool, args } -> { allowed, requires_approval }.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: Orchestration and governance are two distinct services: the orchestrator decides which tool/sub-agent to call next; the governance layer independently decides whether that specific call is allowed, without trusting the orchestrator's own judgment as the enforcement boundary.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    AGENT[Orchestrator] --> GATE[Governance gate]
    GATE -->|allowed| TOOL[Tool execution]
    GATE -->|needs approval| HUMAN[Human approval]
    GATE -->|denied| REJECT[Rejected]
    KILL[Kill-switch] -.->|halts in-flight plan| AGENT
strong_answer.deep_dives: Agent-to-agent delegation should be a first-class case -- one agent discovering and calling another's capability through a real discovery mechanism (an agent-card-style endpoint), not just in-process function calls, since that's the shape real multi-agent systems take once more than one team builds agents independently. The kill-switch specifically needs to halt an in-flight multi-step plan, not just block the next single call -- a governance gate that only checks the next action can't stop a plan that's already mid-execution when a budget or safety threshold breaches.

weak_answer.requirements: Store features so both training and serving can use them.
weak_answer.core_entities: A features table with entity ID and value columns.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: One shared database serves both training queries and live serving lookups.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd add a cache if lookups get slow.

strong_answer.requirements: Support online serving (low-latency lookup of current feature values) and offline training (point-in-time-correct historical feature values, not just the latest value) from one shared feature definition, to avoid training-serving skew.
strong_answer.core_entities: Feature definition, Feature value (entity_id, timestamp, value), Training dataset snapshot (a point-in-time join across many features), Online feature cache, Corpus/feature snapshot hash for reproducibility.
strong_answer.api_interface: GetOnlineFeatures(entity_id) for serving; GetHistoricalFeatures(entity_ids, timestamps) for training, joining each feature as-of its specific timestamp, not the latest value.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: Separate an online store (low-latency KV store) from an offline store (columnar, supports point-in-time joins), fed by the same feature computation logic so training and serving can never silently diverge.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    RAW[Raw events] --> COMPUTE[Feature computation]
    COMPUTE --> ONLINE[(Online store)]
    COMPUTE --> OFFLINE[(Offline store, point-in-time)]
    SERVE[Serving] --> ONLINE
    TRAIN[Training] --> OFFLINE
strong_answer.deep_dives: Point-in-time correctness is the core hard problem: naively joining 'latest value' for each feature during training leaks future information the model wouldn't have had at prediction time. Reproducibility needs to be a content-hash contract over both the transformation logic and the input snapshot, not just 'we track lineage' as a policy statement -- a documented real bug showed object reconstruction during a pipeline stage can silently drop lineage fields to defaults. Freshness vs. compute cost is a real trade-off mapped per feature category: event-driven incremental computation for frequently-changing features, scheduled batch recomputation for expensive slow-changing ones.

weak_answer.requirements: Check user input and model output for bad content and block it.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Run every message through a classifier before and after the LLM call, and block anything flagged.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd tune the classifier threshold if it's too strict or too loose.

strong_answer.requirements: Redact sensitive input (PII, payment tokens) before it reaches the model, flag destructive-action requests for human approval rather than blocking outright, and validate that every output claim is grounded in an actual citation, not fabricated.
strong_answer.core_entities: GuardrailResult (allowed, flags, redacted_text), Input redaction rule, Destructive-action pattern, Citation set, Answer (with grounded flag and risk_flags).
strong_answer.api_interface: inspect_input(text) -> GuardrailResult; validate_output(answer, context) -> Answer with grounded: bool and risk_flags.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: Two distinct guardrail passes: input inspection (redact PII/payment data, flag destructive-action requests for human approval) and output validation (check every claim traces to an actual citation in the retrieved context, flag missing or unknown citations).
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    IN[User input] --> REDACT[Redact PII/payment]
    REDACT --> FLAG[Flag destructive actions]
    FLAG --> LLM[Model call]
    LLM --> VALIDATE[Validate citations]
    VALIDATE --> OUT[Grounded answer or flagged]
strong_answer.deep_dives: The real, working guardrail split is input redaction vs. output grounding validation -- not a single upfront/downstream classifier. Destructive-action requests (delete, refund, wire, disable account) should route to human-approval flags, not outright blocking, since the action might be legitimate. Output validation checks which citation IDs the answer actually references against the ones it was given -- missing citations or references to unknown citation IDs are both real, distinct failure modes worth flagging separately, not collapsed into one generic 'ungrounded' flag. Fail-open vs. fail-closed is a per-category decision, not one global policy: a payment or destructive-action category should fail closed (block and escalate on guardrail-service downtime), while a low-risk tone/style category can fail open, since blocking all traffic on a guardrail outage is its own outage. The human review queue is a first-class part of the system, not a fallback bolted onto the automated pipeline -- it needs its own SLA per risk category, a UI that shows reviewers the redacted input and the specific flag reason rather than just 'flagged', and a feedback loop where reviewer decisions retrain the classifier, otherwise the queue silently backs up and becomes the real bottleneck nobody designed for.

weak_answer.requirements: Return relevant items for a user's query or profile.
weak_answer.core_entities: An item, a user, an embedding.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Embed all items and the query, then return the closest matches by similarity search.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd retrain the embedding model periodically.

strong_answer.requirements: Return a ranked list drawn from hundreds of millions to billions of items within a tens-of-milliseconds budget, support multiple modalities (text/image/video) with a unified relevance signal, and include diversity/exploration so pure engagement-maximization doesn't crowd out everything else.
strong_answer.core_entities: Item (modality-specific embeddings, metadata), User profile, Candidate set (retrieved via ANN index), Ranking score, Exploration budget.
strong_answer.api_interface: GET /recommendations?user_id=&limit= -> { items: [{item_id, score}] }
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: A two-stage pipeline: cheap, high-recall candidate generation via an ANN index over the full catalog, followed by an expensive, high-precision ranking model over the small candidate set -- the same retrieve-then-rank shape as RAG, generalized.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    Q[User request] --> GEN[Candidate generation: ANN index]
    GEN --> MERGE[Merge multiple sources]
    MERGE --> RANK[Ranking model]
    RANK --> DIV[Diversity/exploration pass]
strong_answer.deep_dives: At billion-item scale, brute-force distance computation is infeasible -- an approximate nearest-neighbor index (IVF-PQ or HNSW) is required, with recall/latency/memory as three independently tunable axes against a concrete target (e.g., recall@100 above 90% at p99 under 20ms), not a black box. Position bias is the real, measurable mechanism behind engagement feedback loops -- higher-ranked items get more clicks regardless of true relevance -- corrected via inverse-propensity weighting or randomized exploration slices in training data, not just an exploration budget at serving time.

weak_answer.requirements: Log what the model does and check quality with a test set.
weak_answer.core_entities: A log entry, a test case.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Run a set of test prompts through the model periodically and check the outputs look reasonable.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd add more test cases over time.

strong_answer.requirements: Every production call should produce an async trace (inputs, outputs, intermediate steps) without adding latency to the request path, and a versioned golden suite should be runnable as a real CI merge gate producing pass/fail, not just a manual quality check.
strong_answer.core_entities: Trace (spans per step, cost, latency), Eval suite (versioned, locked test cases), Eval case (input + expect block), Eval run (pass/fail per case against a specific target).
strong_answer.api_interface: POST /eval/run { suite_id, target_base_url } -> { results: [{case_id, passed, detail}] }; GET /traces?run_id= -> full trace tree.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: Tracing (what actually happened in production) and evaluation (does the system still pass known cases) are related but distinct systems -- a common weak design conflates them into one logging system that does neither job well.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    PROD[Production request] -.->|async| TRACE[(Trace store)]
    SUITE[Golden suites] --> RUNNER[Scorer]
    RUNNER --> TARGET[Real service under test]
    RUNNER --> GATE[CI merge gate]
strong_answer.deep_dives: Fixtures that validate (schema-correct) are a different claim than fixtures that actually gate a real build -- only real execution against a live target proves the second one; a real, disclosed example found a genuine fixture bug on its very first real execution. Cost should be a first-class evaluation dimension alongside quality, not a separate finance report -- a model change that improves quality but doubles cost per request is a trade-off, not an unambiguous win, and needs to be visible in the same evaluation output.

weak_answer.requirements: Fine-tune the model on labeled data and then align it with human preferences.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Run supervised fine-tuning, then run RLHF on top, and deploy the final model.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd monitor training loss to make sure it's converging.

strong_answer.requirements: Every training run must produce a versioned, independently evaluable checkpoint (not just a final model), support rollback to a previous checkpoint on eval/safety regression, and treat multi-node fault tolerance and cost attribution as first-class, not afterthoughts.
strong_answer.core_entities: SFT checkpoint, Preference dataset (with provenance: human vs. AI judge), Reward model (independently versioned and evaluated), Policy checkpoint.
strong_answer.api_interface: POST /training/sft -> checkpoint_id; POST /training/reward-model -> reward_model_id + eval_scores; POST /training/rlhf -> new_checkpoint_id + eval_scores.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: The pipeline is a staged sequence with an independent evaluation gate at each stage, not one monolithic job -- a bug in the reward model (the middle stage) needs to be caught before it's baked into an expensive-to-retrain policy model.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    SFT[SFT] --> SFTCKPT[SFT checkpoint]
    PREF[Preference data] --> RM[Reward model]
    RM --> RMEVAL[Reward model eval]
    SFTCKPT --> RLHF[RLHF/DPO]
    RMEVAL --> RLHF
    RLHF --> GATE[Eval + safety gate]
strong_answer.deep_dives: Reward hacking (the policy exploiting a stable reward model's known weakness) is distinct from reward model staleness (the reward model's own reliability silently degrading as the policy's output distribution drifts from what it was validated against) -- the fix for staleness is periodic reward-model refresh against the current policy's actual outputs, not a one-time upfront validation. Real GPU-hour cost attribution per run/stage needs to be visible in real time, the same discipline as real per-call cost metering elsewhere in the stack, not discovered after the fact in a finance review.

weak_answer.requirements: Multiple customers share the platform, each with their own API key.
weak_answer.api_interface: POST /v1/completions with an API key header.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Each tenant gets an API key and a rate limit; requests are routed to a shared pool of servers.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd increase the rate limit for bigger customers.

strong_answer.requirements: Support per-tenant customization (fine-tunes, system prompts) without a fully separate deployment per tenant, and ensure no tenant's traffic pattern degrades another's latency -- which is a harder problem here than typical multi-tenancy because GPU batching means tenants can share the same physical compute at the request level.
strong_answer.core_entities: Tenant (API key, TPM/RPM quota), Model variant (base model + tenant customization layer), Capacity tier (shared vs. dedicated/PTU-style), Quota ledger.
strong_answer.api_interface: POST /v1/completions with tenant API key -> 200 or 429 with retry_after, quota checked before scheduling, not after.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: Quota enforcement happens at the gate, before scheduling, not as post-hoc billing reconciliation -- a rejected request should never consume GPU capacity.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    T1[Tenant A] --> GATE[Auth + quota gate]
    T2[Tenant B] --> GATE
    GATE -->|within quota| SCHED[Shared GPU scheduler]
    GATE -->|dedicated tier| DED[Dedicated pool]
    GATE -->|over quota| REJECT[429]
strong_answer.deep_dives: Per-tenant rate limits alone don't fully solve isolation -- continuous batching multiplexes multiple tenants' requests into the same GPU forward pass, so isolation has to be reasoned about inside the scheduler itself, not just at the API gateway; dedicated capacity tiers exist specifically for tenants needing an isolation guarantee stronger than quota alone provides. Customization should be expressed as a layer (a fine-tuned adapter, an injected system prompt) applied at request time over shared base weights, not a fully forked deployment per tenant -- that doesn't scale operationally past a handful of tenants.

weak_answer.requirements: Run the agent's code in a container so it can't harm the host.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Each task gets its own Docker container that's destroyed after it finishes, with limited network access.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd block access to sensitive files.

strong_answer.requirements: Code execution must be isolated even from adversarial code (standard containers share the host kernel, so a container escape compromises the host directly), and the system must defend against prompt injection -- an instruction hidden in fetched content that tries to hijack the agent's next action, which is a fundamentally different threat from validating direct user input.
strong_answer.core_entities: Sandbox instance (isolated environment, torn down after the task), Tool call (checked against an allow-list before execution), Tool result (treated as untrusted input), Policy (allow-list of commands/network/filesystem).
strong_answer.api_interface: POST /agent/execute { task, allowed_tools, policy_id } -> sandbox_id; POST /sandbox/{id}/tool_call -> result or denied.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: Every tool call passes through an allow-list check before execution, and every tool result flows back into the agent's context as untrusted input, scanned the same as the original tool call, not accepted as trusted reasoning context.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart TB
    AGENT[Agent] --> CALL[Tool call]
    CALL --> POLICY[Allow-list check]
    POLICY -->|denied| REJECT[Rejected]
    POLICY -->|allowed| SANDBOX[gVisor/microVM sandbox]
    SANDBOX --> RESULT[Tool result]
    RESULT -->|untrusted| AGENT
strong_answer.deep_dives: gVisor (intercepts syscalls in user space) or a full microVM (Firecracker-style, hardware-level virtualization) are real isolation choices beyond a standard container -- Anthropic's own documented choice for Claude Code is gVisor, trading full-VM overhead for a smaller attack surface than a shared kernel. The core design principle: even with perfect sandboxing, prompt injection attacks the agent's reasoning, not the execution environment -- the sandbox's allow-list, not the agent's own judgment, must be the actual enforcement boundary, because a successful injection means the agent's judgment is exactly what's compromised.

weak_answer.requirements: Run simple requests on the device and send complex ones to the cloud.
weak_answer.core_entities: An on-device model, a cloud model.
weak_answer.api_interface: LocalInfer(request) or CloudInfer(request), chosen by request complexity.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: A small model on the device handles easy requests; anything else is sent to a cloud model.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd make the cloud tier faster if latency is an issue.

strong_answer.requirements: Simple, latency-sensitive requests should work fully offline on-device; requests exceeding on-device capability should transparently escalate to a cloud tier whose privacy guarantees are as strong as architecturally possible -- not just encrypted in transit, but designed so the cloud tier can't retain or inspect the request at all.
strong_answer.core_entities: On-device model (small, quantized), Escalation classifier (fast, on-device), Cloud tier (ephemeral, no-retention), Session context.
strong_answer.api_interface: LocalInfer(request, context) -> result or escalate; CloudInfer(request, context, privacy_policy: ephemeral_no_retention) -> result.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: Escalating to the cloud tier is not the same privacy trade-off as a typical cloud API call -- the design goal is that the cloud tier processes the request and discards it, with no operator visibility into content, as close to on-device guarantees as architecturally possible (Apple's real, published Private Cloud Compute design).
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    REQ[Request] --> CLASSIFY[On-device classifier]
    CLASSIFY -->|within capability| LOCAL[On-device model]
    CLASSIFY -->|exceeds capability| CLOUD[Cloud tier: ephemeral, no retention]
strong_answer.deep_dives: A no-retention cloud tier is an architectural guarantee, not a policy statement -- it requires verifiable non-persistence by construction (no durable storage the request ever touches), no privileged operator access path, and ideally cryptographic attestation of what code is actually running, not just 'we delete it after 30 days.' The escalation classifier's boundary should be trained against the on-device model's actual known failure modes (multi-step reasoning, world-knowledge gaps), not a generic token-count threshold, since a crude proxy escalates the wrong requests.

weak_answer.requirements: Track where training data came from in case there's a legal question later.
weak_answer.core_entities: A data source, a training example.
weak_answer.api_interface: GET /provenance?source= -> list of examples from that source.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Tag each training example with its source when we ingest it.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd remove a source from future training runs if there's a legal issue.

strong_answer.requirements: Every training example needs source, license, and consent status tracked through the full pipeline (ingestion, dedup, filtering, synthetic augmentation) without being silently dropped, and the system needs to support answering 'what did this model train on' as a real, auditable query, not a research project.
strong_answer.core_entities: Source (license terms, consent/opt-in status), Training example (source, content-hash), Corpus snapshot (versioned, immutable, linked to the training run that used it), Provenance query.
strong_answer.api_interface: POST /corpus/ingest { source_id, examples } -> rejects examples missing required consent/license metadata; GET /provenance/query?source_id= -> training examples + models trained.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: Provenance is enforced at ingestion (reject data missing required metadata, don't accept-and-hope-to-reconstruct-later) and preserved end-to-end via a corpus-snapshot-to-model linkage.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    RAW[Raw sources] --> GATE[Ingestion gate: license/consent check]
    GATE -->|missing metadata| REJECT[Rejected]
    GATE -->|passes| CORPUS[(Versioned corpus snapshot)]
    CORPUS --> TRAIN[Training run]
    TRAIN --> MODEL[Model, linked to snapshot]
strong_answer.deep_dives: The harder question a corpus-removal answer misses: can you prove an already-trained model doesn't retain extractable traces of removed data? Membership-inference testing (probing the model for verbatim/near-verbatim reproduction of disputed-source samples) is the real, concrete mechanism -- corpus-level removal alone doesn't resolve exposure already baked into a shipped model. Synthetic (model-generated) training data needs its own provenance dimension distinct from human-authored data, tracking generation lineage, given real model-collapse research on recursively training on prior models' output.

weak_answer.requirements: Save the agent's progress periodically so it can resume after a crash.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Save the conversation history to a database periodically and reload it on restart.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd check a log before repeating an action to avoid duplicates.

strong_answer.requirements: A long-running agent must survive a process restart mid-task without repeating already-completed side effects, support human-in-the-loop pauses lasting minutes to days without holding a compute resource open, and provide mid-flight observability so a human can inspect and cancel an in-progress task safely.
strong_answer.core_entities: Task (status, execution history), Checkpoint (durable snapshot sufficient to resume exactly), Side-effect record (keyed by idempotency ID), Human checkpoint (open-ended pause, no held resource).
strong_answer.api_interface: POST /tasks -> task_id; POST /tasks/{id}/resume_with_human_input; POST /tasks/{id}/cancel.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: The real, robust pattern is durable-execution-style: reasoning code re-executes from the top on every resume, but every side-effecting operation is wrapped so its result -- not just whether it ran -- is durably recorded, and replay returns the cached result instantly instead of re-running it.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    START[Task starts] --> STEP[Reasoning step]
    STEP --> EFFECT{Side effect?}
    EFFECT -->|yes| CHECK[Check side-effect log]
    CHECK --> EXEC[Execute + record]
    EFFECT -->|no| CKPT[Checkpoint]
    EXEC --> CKPT
    CKPT --> MORE{More steps?}
    MORE -->|human input needed| PAUSE[Pause, no compute held]
strong_answer.deep_dives: The naive full-conversation-replay approach is unsafe the moment the agent takes real side effects, since it re-executes every tool call including consequential ones -- the fix is the durable-execution log itself acting as the checkpoint, not a single frozen-state blob. Human-in-the-loop pauses need to be represented as a fully durable, resource-free state (a database record awaiting an external event) since a pause might last days, and holding a process or locked resource open that long is a real operational hazard.

weak_answer.requirements: Make sure we have enough GPUs for training and serving.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Reserve some GPU capacity and use on-demand instances when we need more.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd buy more reserved capacity if we keep running out.

strong_answer.requirements: Forecast GPU-hour demand separately for training (lumpy, discrete large jobs) and serving (steadier, traffic-driven), and decide procurement mix (reserved, on-demand/spot, secondary provider) per workload with graceful degradation when a pool is exhausted, since reserved capacity has real weeks-to-months lead time.
strong_answer.core_entities: Workload (demand profile, priority), Capacity pool (reserved/on-demand/spot, cost per GPU-hour), Allocation (bound to a pool with a fallback chain), Utilization record.
strong_answer.api_interface: POST /capacity/request { workload_id, gpu_hours_forecast, priority } -> { allocation, fallback_pools, estimated_cost }; GET /capacity/utilization -> reserved/consumed/idle hours.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: A forecaster drives procurement ahead of demand, a scheduler binds workloads against whatever pools exist with an explicit fallback chain (reserved -> on-demand -> secondary provider), and real utilization feeds back into the next forecast.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    FCST[Forecaster] --> PLANNER[Procurement planner]
    PLANNER --> RES[Reserved]
    PLANNER --> OND[On-demand/spot]
    PLANNER --> SEC[Secondary provider]
    WORK[Workload] --> SCHED[Scheduler]
    SCHED --> RES
    SCHED -->|exhausted| OND
    OND -->|exhausted| SEC
strong_answer.deep_dives: This is a portfolio decision across at least three tiers, not a single reserved-vs-on-demand binary -- serving skews reserved (predictable, can't tolerate preemption), training experimentation skews spot (tolerant if checkpointed), and secondary providers exist specifically to remove a single vendor's quota ceiling as a hard constraint on scaling speed. Utilization measurement is a required input to the next forecast, not an afterthought report -- a capacity plan without a real feedback loop can't correct a bad forecast.

weak_answer.requirements: Serve users from multiple regions for low latency and train wherever there's capacity.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Replicate the serving stack in each region and pick whichever region has GPUs for training.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd add more regions if latency is bad somewhere.

strong_answer.requirements: Training region selection is driven by data gravity and residency (where GPU capacity and legally-allowed data co-locate); serving region selection is driven by user proximity and failover -- these are different problems and a design that treats them identically misses the point.
strong_answer.core_entities: Region (GPU capacity profile, residency classification), Training job (bound to a region matching both capacity and residency), Serving replica (regional, with a failover target), Data residency policy.
strong_answer.api_interface: POST /training/schedule { dataset_residency_tag, gpu_requirement } -> region + reason; GET /serving/route?client_region= -> primary + failover region.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: Training-region selection and serving-region selection are two separate decision paths that meet only at the trained-model artifact boundary, since a model is usually not subject to the same residency constraints as its raw training data once training completes.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Training
    DATA[Residency-tagged data] --> TSCHED[Training scheduler]
    TSCHED --> TREGION[Training region]
    end
    subgraph Serving
    USER[User] --> GLB[Global load balancer]
    GLB --> SA[Region A]
    GLB -.->|failover| SB[Region B]
    end
strong_answer.deep_dives: A regulator-mandated residency constraint is real and non-negotiable -- provision capacity in the compliant region even at higher cost, or don't offer that capability to that customer segment, since there's no clever routing trick around a legal boundary. Whether a trained model actually inherits its training data's residency constraint is a testable question, not an assumption -- membership inference auditing checks whether the model can be made to emit specific training examples, and differential privacy (DP-SGD) is the real architectural mitigation with a genuine accuracy-vs-privacy-budget cost, not a free choice.

weak_answer.requirements: If something breaks, roll back to a backup and recover quickly.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Keep backups of the model and roll back to the last one if something goes wrong.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd test the rollback process occasionally.

strong_answer.requirements: Separate RTO/RPO targets by workload -- serving needs near-zero RPO and minutes-scale RTO since it's directly user-facing; model artifact storage needs zero RPO since losing a version means an expensive re-training cycle; training pipelines can tolerate hours-scale RTO with checkpoint-based RPO.
strong_answer.core_entities: Model artifact (versioned, immutable), Deployment (artifact + traffic percentage), Incident (triggers rollback or regional failover), Rollback target (last eval-gate-passed deployment).
strong_answer.api_interface: POST /deploy { model_artifact_id, traffic_pct } -> deployment_id; POST /rollback { deployment_id } -> restored_deployment_id, eta_seconds.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: Two distinct failure classes handled differently: a bad deployment (rollback to last-good, entirely within one region, should be near-instant) versus a regional infrastructure failure (failover to a secondary region running the same last-good model) -- conflating these into one recovery path is the common weak design.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    NEW[New deployment] -->|canary| PROD[Production traffic]
    PROD --> MON[Health/quality monitor]
    MON -->|regression| ROLLBACK[Automatic rollback]
    subgraph Regional DR
    PRIMARY[Primary region] -.->|health fails| SECONDARY[Secondary region]
    end
strong_answer.deep_dives: Rollback to the last known-good model is the actual first line of defense, exercised far more often than regional failover -- an elaborate regional-failover architecture with rollback treated as an afterthought gets the priority backwards. Pre-production eval gating (a real, versioned golden suite gating promotion) is a stronger primary defense than fast post-hoc recovery, since it prevents the bad deployment from ever reaching production traffic at all -- test the DR plan by actually triggering a rollback and failover on a schedule, not just documenting the intended procedure.

weak_answer.requirements: Connect the training nodes with a fast network.
weak_answer.api_interface: N/A -- internal cluster networking, no external API.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Put all training nodes in the same VPC with a fast network connection and use NCCL for GPU communication.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd upgrade the network if bandwidth becomes a bottleneck.

strong_answer.requirements: All-reduce/all-to-all collective operations are synchronous barriers -- every rank waits for the slowest path before the next step starts -- so the network's worst-case latency, not average, sets the actual training step time, and placement needs to be topology-aware, not just fast in aggregate.
strong_answer.core_entities: Node (rack/switch/zone position), Collective operation (all-reduce/all-to-all via NCCL), Topology (bisection bandwidth at each level), Placement (rank-to-node assignment).
strong_answer.api_interface: POST /scheduler/place { job_id, world_size, gpus_per_node } -> rank_to_node_mapping, topology_score.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: RDMA/RoCE or InfiniBand for the training fabric, with topology-aware scheduling co-locating tightly-coupled ranks (tensor-parallel) within a rack while loosely-coupled ranks (data-parallel) can spread across racks or zones.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart TB
    subgraph RackA
    N1[Node] ---|RDMA| N2[Node]
    end
    subgraph RackB
    N3[Node] ---|RDMA| N4[Node]
    end
    N1 -.->|spine, lower bandwidth| N3
    SCHED[Topology-aware scheduler] --> N1
strong_answer.deep_dives: RoCE trades Ethernet's cost/ecosystem advantage for RDMA's latency benefit by bypassing the kernel network stack (Meta's real documented choice); InfiniBand gives the lowest latency at higher hardware cost. The straggler problem is the subtler failure mode: a single degraded, not failed, link can silently slow an entire multi-day run to its slowest participant with no error produced, since there's no request to retry against a synchronous barrier -- this needs per-link health monitoring and automated exclusion of degraded nodes, not post-hoc failure detection.

weak_answer.requirements: Keep secrets in a secret manager and require authentication for sensitive access.
weak_answer.api_interface: N/A -- policy/infra layer, not a public API.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Store API keys in a secret manager, require auth for model weight access, and log who accessed what.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd rotate keys periodically for security.

strong_answer.requirements: Model weights need a categorically different access control from standard secrets -- multi-party authorization, not just stricter single-approver auth -- because a single exfiltration event reproduces the company's entire core product capability, unlike any single-artifact-theft risk in most non-AI software. Compliance certifications (SOC2, HIPAA) are market-access decisions with specific engineering cost, not a general hardening checkbox.
strong_answer.core_entities: Model weights artifact (multi-party-authorized access), Access grant (scoped, auditable, time-bound), Secret (managed store, rotation policy), Compliance boundary.
strong_answer.api_interface: POST /access/request { resource, requestor, justification } -> approved, requires_second_approver, audit_log_id.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: The highest-value asset (model weights) gets a categorically different access control -- multi-party authorization -- not just a stricter version of the same single-approver flow used for lower-value resources (Anthropic's real, published ASL-3 standard).
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart TB
    ENG[Engineer] --> AUTHZ[Authorization layer]
    AUTHZ -->|standard resource| GRANT[Scoped grant]
    AUTHZ -->|model weights| APPROVER2[Second approver required]
    APPROVER2 --> GRANT
    GRANT --> AUDIT[(Audit log)]
strong_answer.deep_dives: The Llama weights leak is the concrete proof this isn't theoretical -- you can rotate a leaked API key, but you cannot meaningfully rotate leaked weights once redistributed. A real operational gotcha: a managed secret store rotating a key's value doesn't guarantee already-running compute picks it up -- a service can keep authenticating with the old key until a forced redeploy, so 'I rotated the key' and 'the old key stopped working' are two different claims that both need verifying (a real deployment found exactly this gap).

weak_answer.requirements: Run containers with autoscaling and use smaller instances to save cost.
weak_answer.api_interface: N/A -- infra orchestration layer, not a public API.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Use Kubernetes with autoscaling; add more pods when load increases.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd use smaller instance types where possible to cut cost.

strong_answer.requirements: GPU bin-packing is coarser-grained and higher-stakes than CPU bin-packing since GPUs generally can't be fractionally shared -- a node with 3 of 8 GPUs allocated costs the same as a fully-packed one, making active consolidation a much higher-value optimization here than for CPU-only workloads.
strong_answer.core_entities: Pod/task (GPU-count and type requirement), Node (fixed GPU count), Cluster autoscaler, idle_allocated_gpus metric (distinct from simply unallocated).
strong_answer.api_interface: POST /schedule { workload_id, gpu_count, gpu_type, priority } -> node_id, wait_time_estimate; GET /cluster/utilization -> total/allocated/idle_allocated_gpus.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: Kubernetes at GPU scale (OpenAI's own documented approach) gives fine-grained scheduling control for sustained workloads, while a managed serverless option gives true scale-to-zero for genuinely bursty ones -- but only real applied-and-torn-down infrastructure, not a terraform plan, proves either claim.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    WORK[Workload] --> SCHED[GPU-aware scheduler]
    SCHED -->|fits| NODE[Existing GPU node]
    SCHED -->|no fit| AUTOSCALE[Cluster autoscaler]
    AUTOSCALE --> NEWNODE[New GPU node]
    NODE --> UTIL[(Utilization tracker)]
    UTIL -.->|idle GPU alert| RIGHTSIZE[Consolidation pass]
strong_answer.deep_dives: Real bugs only a real deployment surfaces, never a terraform plan: a Dockerfile silently never rebuilt after a new dependency was added, a container ignoring a platform-injected PORT variable, a container registry needing force-delete configured before teardown works. The actual evidence someone has real infrastructure experience is that the Terraform was applied, hit a live endpoint doing real work, and was torn down cleanly -- each independently verifiable -- not that it was merely written.

weak_answer.requirements: Limit how many requests a client can make per time window.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Keep a counter per client and reject requests once the counter exceeds the limit, resetting each window.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd increase the limit for premium clients.

strong_answer.requirements: The limiter must work correctly across multiple servers handling the same client's traffic (a per-server in-memory counter undercounts), must avoid the fixed-window boundary-burst flaw, and needs an explicit, stated fail-open vs. fail-closed behavior if the counter store itself is unavailable.
strong_answer.core_entities: Client (applicable limit rules), Limit rule (threshold + window semantics), Counter/bucket (shared across servers via Redis or similar).
strong_answer.api_interface: CheckLimit(client_id, rule_id) -> allowed, remaining, retry_after_seconds.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: A shared counter store (Redis) with an atomic check-and-increment (Lua script) closes the race condition a naive read-check-increment sequence has, where two concurrent requests can both read an under-limit value before either increments it.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    REQ[Request] --> GATE[Rate limit check]
    GATE -->|query| STORE[(Shared counter store)]
    GATE -->|within limit| APP[Application]
    GATE -->|over limit| REJECT[429]
strong_answer.deep_dives: A sliding-window counter or token bucket avoids the fixed-window flaw where a client can send the full limit at the very end of one window and again at the start of the next, doubling the effective rate briefly. For multi-region rate limiting, the real mechanism is either static fractional allocation (zero coordination, wastes capacity under skew) or periodic gossip/reconciliation (recovers accuracy at the cost of a bounded staleness window) -- naming which one and why, with a quantified trade-off, is what separates a strong answer from 'shard it regionally.'

weak_answer.requirements: Users send messages to each other and see them appear in real time.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Use WebSockets to keep a connection open, and push new messages to the recipient if they're online, otherwise store and deliver later.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd add more servers to handle more connections.

strong_answer.requirements: Connection count, not database throughput, is the first-order scaling constraint -- a server hits a connection-count ceiling well before a database bottleneck -- and delivery must be at-least-once with client-side deduplication, since exactly-once at the server alone is either impossible or far more overhead than it's worth.
strong_answer.core_entities: Message (client-generated ID for dedup), Conversation, Connection (mapped to which server holds it), Presence record.
strong_answer.api_interface: Connect() -> persistent connection; SendMessage(conversation_id, content, client_message_id) -> pushed to recipient with delivery_status.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: Because sender and recipient are often on different gateway servers, a stateful routing layer is required to know which server currently holds which user's connection -- a materially different problem than stateless request/response.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    C1[Client A] --> GW1[Gateway 1]
    C2[Client B] --> GW2[Gateway 2]
    GW1 --> ROUTER[Connection router]
    ROUTER -->|B on GW2| GW2
    GW1 --> QUEUE[(Per-user ordered queue)]
strong_answer.deep_dives: WhatsApp's real published architecture leans on Erlang/BEAM's lightweight-process model specifically to maximize connections per machine, since that's the actual first-order bottleneck. Offline delivery needs per-recipient sequence numbers (Meta's real 'Iris' pattern) so a reconnecting client can request 'everything since sequence N' and get a gap-free, ordered backlog -- a raw pub/sub fan-out doesn't guarantee this across a disconnect/reconnect cycle.

weak_answer.requirements: Show posts from people a user follows, ranked by relevance.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: When someone posts, push it to all their followers' feed caches, and show the feed in reverse-chronological or relevance order.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd tune the ranking algorithm over time.

strong_answer.requirements: Read-heavy at extreme scale, so read-path efficiency dominates, and the design needs an explicit strategy for accounts with hundreds of millions of followers, since naive fan-out-on-write would mean one post triggers a catastrophic number of writes.
strong_answer.core_entities: Post (engagement signals), Follow graph, Feed entry (user, post, relevance score), Ranking model.
strong_answer.api_interface: GetFeed(user_id, cursor) -> ranked posts; CreatePost(author_id, content) -> post_id, fan_out_status.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: A hybrid fan-out strategy by author type: fan-out-on-write (push to follower feed caches) for normal accounts, fan-out-on-read (compute at request time) for celebrity/huge-follower accounts, merged at aggregation time (Meta's real, published Aggregator/Leaf/Tailer pattern).
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart TB
    POST[New post] --> FANOUT{Fan-out decision}
    FANOUT -->|normal| WRITE[Push to feed caches]
    FANOUT -->|celebrity| SKIP[Skip write fan-out]
    REQ[GetFeed] --> AGG[Aggregator]
    AGG --> WRITE
    AGG -->|celebrity posts| CELEB[(Celebrity index)]
strong_answer.deep_dives: Pure fan-out-on-write breaks down for a 100M-follower account -- one post would require 100M+ individual cache writes, which is why real systems specifically design the hybrid split. Ranking is a genuinely separate, multi-stage process (cheap candidate generation, then an expensive ranking model, then diversity/integrity adjustments), and position bias needs correction via inverse-propensity weighting or randomized exploration, not just a diversity penalty bolted onto the final sort.

weak_answer.requirements: Run scheduled jobs on a fleet of workers and retry if a worker fails.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: A scheduler checks a database for due jobs and dispatches them to available workers.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd retry a job if a worker crashes.

strong_answer.requirements: True exactly-once execution is not achievable across a network that can partition -- the real target is at-least-once execution plus idempotency -- and the scheduler itself must not be a single point of failure, since a scheduler crash shouldn't halt all jobs until manually restarted.
strong_answer.core_entities: Job definition (schedule, retry policy), Job execution (time-bounded lease), Lease, Worker.
strong_answer.api_interface: ScheduleJob(cron_expr, job_spec, retry_policy) -> job_id; GET /jobs/{id}/executions -> status per execution.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: The scheduler runs as a small Paxos/Raft-based consensus cluster (Google's real, documented distributed cron design) -- one active leader firing jobs, standbys ready to take over, with consensus guaranteeing at most one leader is ever recognized by a majority.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    DEF[Job definitions] --> LEADER[Scheduler leader]
    LEADER -->|consensus| STANDBY[Standby schedulers]
    LEADER --> QUEUE[(Execution queue)]
    QUEUE --> WORKER[Worker: leases execution]
    WORKER -->|lease expires| REASSIGN[Reassign]
strong_answer.deep_dives: A worker holds a time-bounded lease; if it expires without completion, the execution is reassigned -- meaning a job can genuinely run twice if the original worker was just slow, not dead, so idempotency is structural, not optional. An explicit backlog-catch-up policy after an extended outage matters too -- running overdue jobs in priority order, and treating some as stale enough that running them late is actively wrong, as a per-job configuration, not a scheduler-wide default.

weak_answer.requirements: Generate unique IDs across multiple machines without collisions.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Use a central counter service that hands out the next ID, or fall back to random UUIDs.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd add more counter service replicas if it becomes a bottleneck.

strong_answer.requirements: IDs must be unique with zero coordination on the hot path (a centralized counter recreates the exact single-bottleneck problem a distributed system is trying to avoid) and should be roughly sortable by creation time for pagination, while correctly handling clock skew across generator nodes.
strong_answer.core_entities: Generator node (assigned node ID, out of band), ID (timestamp + node ID + sequence bits, Snowflake-style structure).
strong_answer.api_interface: GenerateID() -> uint64, no network call on the hot path.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: Uniqueness by construction: each node's assigned ID is baked into every ID it generates, so as long as node IDs are unique and each node's per-millisecond sequence counter doesn't overflow, no coordination is needed between nodes at request time.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart TB
    subgraph ID structure
    TS[41 bits: timestamp] --> NODE[10 bits: node ID]
    NODE --> SEQ[12 bits: sequence]
    end
    GEN[Generator node] --> ID[64-bit unique ID]
strong_answer.deep_dives: A structural, not reactive, fix for clock skew: a Hybrid Logical Clock combines physical time with a logical counter that only ever increases, guaranteeing monotonicity per node regardless of physical clock behavior, and also solving cross-node causal ordering -- a stronger answer than 'detect a backward jump and buffer.' Worth naming plainly: Snowflake is a real Twitter system, not something any of these six companies invented -- don't misattribute it.

weak_answer.requirements: Multiple users can edit the same document at the same time.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Apply each user's edits to the document in the order they arrive at the server.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd handle conflicts by whoever saved last.

strong_answer.requirements: Concurrent edits to the same or overlapping regions must converge to the same final state for every user, not diverge -- last-write-wins silently loses data the moment two users edit overlapping regions concurrently, which is the exact case that needs to be handled correctly, not just the common non-overlapping case.
strong_answer.core_entities: Document (operation history), Operation (insert/delete at a position), Client state (version marker).
strong_answer.api_interface: ApplyOperation(doc_id, operation, client_version) -> transformed_operation, new_version, broadcast to other clients.
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: Operational transformation (server-mediated, requires a central sequencing authority) or CRDTs (peer-to-peer capable, no central authority needed) are the two real answers -- the transform function itself, adjusting one operation's position indices to account for a concurrent one already applied, is the actual hard part, not the high-level 'let users edit together' concept.
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    C1[Client A types] --> LOCAL1[Local state, optimistic]
    C1 --> SERVER[OT/CRDT server]
    C2[Client B types] --> SERVER
    SERVER -->|transform| TRANSFORMED[Transformed ops]
    TRANSFORMED --> LOCAL1
    TRANSFORMED --> LOCAL2[Local state B]
strong_answer.deep_dives: CRDTs have a real, often-omitted operational cost: tombstones (markers for deleted content) must be retained for every deletion in a naive implementation, since a late-arriving concurrent operation from an offline peer might still reference that position -- real systems need explicit tombstone garbage collection bounded by the oldest still-syncing peer's state, not unbounded retention. The OT-vs-CRDT choice should map to a real product requirement: offline-first support favors CRDTs; an always-online product can accept OT's central-server dependency.

weak_answer.requirements: Cache frequently-accessed data to reduce load on the database.
weak_answer.high_level_design_text: Check the cache first; on a miss, query the database and populate the cache with a TTL.
weak_answer.deep_dives: We'd increase the TTL if the cache hit rate is too low.

strong_answer.requirements: TTL-only invalidation isn't sufficient -- a write followed by an immediate read can still return stale data for the rest of the TTL window unless writes trigger explicit invalidation -- and the design must scale across multiple axes: within a cluster, across clusters in a region, and across regions, not just add more cache servers.
strong_answer.core_entities: Cache entry (key, value, TTL), Cache cluster (consistent-hashed), Origin/backing store, Edge location (for the CDN variant).
strong_answer.api_interface: Get(key) -> value, hit: bool; Set(key, value, ttl); Invalidate(key).
strong_answer.high_level_design_text: Write-triggered invalidation alongside TTL, with consistent hashing distributing keys across cache servers, and a real multi-axis scaling story: within-cluster, cross-cluster regional pools, and cross-region replication (Meta's real, published Memcache-at-scale architecture).
strong_answer.high_level_design_mermaid:
flowchart LR
    REQ[Request] --> CACHE{Cache lookup}
    CACHE -->|hit| RETURN[Return cached value]
    CACHE -->|miss| ORIGIN[(Origin)]
    ORIGIN --> POPULATE[Populate cache]
    WRITE[Write] --> INVALIDATE[Invalidate entry]
strong_answer.deep_dives: The thundering herd problem -- many concurrent requests missing simultaneously when a popular entry expires or a node fails, all hammering the origin at once -- is mitigated with request coalescing (the first miss populates the cache, concurrent requests wait on that result) or jittered TTLs so entries don't all expire at once. Cross-region cache invalidation is a genuinely hard trade-off: synchronous propagation adds write latency; asynchronous leaves a real staleness window -- naming this trade-off explicitly, the way Meta's real architecture had to, is what a senior-only answer misses.

weak_answer.situation: Our team had a lot of manual work reviewing support tickets.
weak_answer.task: We wanted to use AI to handle more of it.
weak_answer.action: I built a bot that used an LLM to auto-reply to tickets.
weak_answer.result: We reduced the team size a bit and things seemed to go okay.
weak_answer.follow_up_response: We just watched it for a while and it seemed fine, so we kept scaling it up.

strong_answer.situation: Our e-commerce ops team of 8 was manually triaging return requests -- checking item-condition claims against photos, matching them to order history, and deciding refund vs. replacement vs. deny -- at a volume that couldn't scale with order growth without adding headcount every quarter.
strong_answer.task: Automate the bulk of these decisions without introducing fraud risk or customer-trust damage from a wrong call, since a bad automated refund decision is a real financial and reputational cost, not just an inconvenience.
strong_answer.action: Built a pipeline that checked three distinct things per case, not one blended 'looks legit' pass: structural correctness (did the claim include the required photo/order-ID pair the schema needed), grounding (did the claimed item condition match what the photo actually showed, not just what the customer wrote), and risk-tier (order value, customer return history, and whether the item was flagged high-fraud-category). Only the risk-tier signal decided whether a case auto-resolved or routed to a human -- low-value, low-risk-history cases resolved automatically; anything above a dollar or history threshold got a human-approval gate, regardless of how confident the model was.
strong_answer.result: Manual triage headcount went from 8 to 3, with the automated share handling roughly 75% of volume; the 3 remaining staff handled the flagged 25% plus spot-audited a sample of automated decisions weekly against the risk-tier logs.
strong_answer.follow_up_response: We didn't scale the automated share based on a gut feeling that it was working -- we tracked pass rates separately per risk tier (not one blended accuracy number) plus a weekly audit trail review of every gated decision, and only expanded the auto-resolve threshold as those tier-specific numbers held up over several weeks of real volume. The headcount number was a consequence of that evidence, not a target we were automating toward.

weak_answer.situation: We had a blog post claiming our system was cost-efficient.
weak_answer.task: I checked if that was actually true.
weak_answer.action: I looked at the code and found some things that weren't quite right, so I fixed them.
weak_answer.result: The numbers are more accurate now.
weak_answer.follow_up_response: I just went with the first fix that came to mind.

strong_answer.situation: I'd publicly claimed in a conference talk that our recommendation service's per-request cost was fully instrumented and used to drive infra decisions -- a strong, checkable claim that nobody was actively pressuring me to re-verify.
strong_answer.task: Audit the actual code behind that claim across the two services that fed the dashboard I'd shown, rather than assume the talk's claim still held months later.
strong_answer.action: Read the real request-handling code, not just the dashboard. Found that one service's 'cost per request' was computed from a fixed per-model rate card that hadn't been updated in two pricing cycles, and the other estimated token counts from response character length even though the real API response already contained exact token counts that were being discarded. My first-draft fix was to patch each service's estimate independently, but mid-plan I realized that wouldn't produce a trustworthy cross-service total -- two services each computing 'their own' corrected number still couldn't answer 'what's our real combined spend' without one shared source of truth. I built a small shared metering library both services called instead, verified it against a live instance with real traffic before switching either service over.
strong_answer.result: Both services now report real, metered cost instead of stale or guessed numbers, and the audit itself became evidence for the original claim -- a self-audit with a shipped fix, not just a restated assertion.
strong_answer.follow_up_response: I didn't patch the two services independently because the actual capability that mattered -- a trustworthy cross-service total -- structurally requires one shared ledger; two independently-corrected estimates still can't be reconciled after the fact. That requirement wasn't in my original plan; I changed the approach once I saw it rather than shipping the faster, locally-correct-but-globally-useless fix.

weak_answer.situation: I found an endpoint that didn't require a login.
weak_answer.action: I added an authentication check to that endpoint.
weak_answer.follow_up_response: I don't think it's an issue anywhere else.

strong_answer.situation: While reviewing one microservice for a security questionnaire, I noticed its internal 'trigger batch job' endpoint had no caller authentication at all -- anyone with the URL could kick off an expensive job.
strong_answer.task: Rather than just patch that one endpoint, find out how widespread this exact pattern was across the six services our team owned and fix it consistently, without breaking the zero-config local dev experience every service's README promised.
strong_answer.action: Audited all six services specifically for this pattern and found it in four more, including one where the same gap existed independently in both its main API and a separate serverless function -- the same mistake made twice in two different runtimes for the same service. Fixed all five with one consistent pattern: an API-key check that's enforced only when the key environment variable is actually set in that deployment. Unset means open, so local/dev usage never broke, but every real deployment got real protection by setting one variable. While fixing the service with the widest blast radius, I also noticed a second, adjacent gap -- the caller's claimed identity in the request body was never actually verified against anything -- and flagged it explicitly in that service's own risk register rather than letting the auth fix imply a completeness it didn't have.
strong_answer.result: All five services gained the same consistent, opt-in auth gate, documented in each service's own deploy docs next to the environment variable to set; the adjacent identity-spoofing risk was disclosed rather than silently left for someone to discover later.
strong_answer.follow_up_response: The fix wasn't just the code change -- every service's README status table and deploy docs were updated in the same commit to call out exactly which environment variable to set before a real deployment, so the gap between 'secure by default' and 'secure once someone remembers to configure it' is visible in the same place someone would look before shipping, not buried in a commit history nobody reads before deploying.

weak_answer.situation: Our payment integration was old and our EDI vendor was expensive.
weak_answer.action: I moved us to a new payment provider and stopped using the EDI vendor.

strong_answer.situation: Our checkout only supported one payment gateway, which didn't have great coverage or reliability in a region we were expanding into, and our inventory-sync integration ran through a licensed EDI translator that we had zero visibility or control into -- every schema change from a trading partner became a vendor support ticket with its own cost and multi-week lead time.
strong_answer.task: Add real regional payment capability that could scale to future regions, and bring the EDI translation logic in-house so schema changes became internal work instead of vendor tickets -- accepting more short-term migration complexity for long-term ownership.
strong_answer.action: For payments, built a gateway-abstraction boundary so each region's specific gateway is a pluggable adapter behind one common transaction interface -- a region-specific quirk (currency handling, local payment methods) lives in that region's adapter, not as conditional logic scattered through the core checkout flow. For EDI, stopped treating trading-partner documents as an opaque format only the vendor's black-box translator understood; built an internal parser that turned those documents into the same domain events the rest of the inventory system already consumed, so a new trading-partner requirement became a code change we owned, not a vendor ticket.
strong_answer.result: The new region's payment success rate improved measurably and future regions could be added by writing one new adapter instead of a new integration project; EDI-related vendor costs dropped substantially and schema changes that used to take weeks became days, handled by our own team.
strong_answer.follow_up_response: The faster path in both cases was more vendor lock-in with a hard ceiling -- another region-specific gateway integration that wouldn't extend to the next region without redoing the work, or continuing to depend entirely on a vendor's translator and support cycle for every schema change. The complexity we accepted upfront was specifically the kind that pays down over time (an owned boundary, an owned parsing layer), not the kind that just accumulates, which is what made it worth defending against pressure to just ship the quicker integration.

weak_answer.situation: We wanted to build a new AI product from scratch.
weak_answer.task: I had to figure out what to build.
weak_answer.action: I built as many features as I could think of and shipped it.
weak_answer.follow_up_response: I think we got most of it right.

strong_answer.situation: Asked to build a 0-to-1 internal tool that would draft and post content across several external channels on a team's behalf, with the ambiguity being in the scope itself -- how much should actually auto-publish versus require a human, and which channels were even worth real integration effort.
strong_answer.task: Ship something real rather than a demo, avoiding both a fully autonomous publisher nobody had actually asked for (a real compliance risk) and a polished-looking demo where 'publish' was fake.
strong_answer.action: Made one structural decision early and never treated it as configurable: every irreversible action -- anything that posted publicly -- gated behind explicit human approval, enforced at the workflow-graph level, not as a settings toggle someone could quietly disable. The harder call was scope: two of the five target channels had a real, stable publish API; the other three didn't have anything comparable. Rather than fake auto-publish for those three in a way that would break silently later, I shipped real OAuth-based publishing only for the two real channels and disclosed the other three as a manual copy-export, stated plainly in the product itself rather than hidden in documentation.
strong_answer.result: A real, running tool with genuine automated publishing to two channels, honest disclosed limitations on three, and a governance gate that couldn't be quietly bypassed -- shipped faster than trying to fake full automation everywhere would have allowed, and without the trust risk of a pipeline that silently mishandled channels it didn't really support.
strong_answer.follow_up_response: A stakeholder did push for all five channels to auto-publish at launch. My answer was that faking auto-publish on a channel without a real, stable API creates a worse failure mode than disclosing the limitation -- a pipeline that silently breaks or posts malformed content on three of five channels is a bigger trust and compliance risk than a product that's transparent about doing two things well and three as a manual-friendly export. Scoping down to what was real, and disclosing the gap instead of hiding it, held up even under pressure to look more complete on paper.

weak_answer.framework: Cost, latency, and safety are all important and you have to balance them. It depends on the situation.
weak_answer.applied_example: Sometimes you care more about one than the others.

strong_answer.framework: These three don't trade off as a fixed triangle where you permanently pick two -- the right answer changes per decision point within the same system, and the skill is naming which axis is non-negotiable for this specific call. Safety wins when the action is irreversible or customer-facing at scale (a refund, an external message) -- these get a human-in-the-loop gate regardless of latency cost, because a bad autonomous action is asymmetrically worse than the cumulative cost of approvals. Latency wins when the user is waiting synchronously and the action itself is low-risk -- a synchronous answer shouldn't wait on a policy check meant for a different, higher-risk part of the pipeline. Cost only becomes the deciding axis once safety and latency are already satisfied and volume is the remaining variable -- and it needs a real, metered number to optimize against, not a guessed one.
strong_answer.applied_example: In a support-automation pipeline I worked on, the refund-issuance path kept a human-approval gate even after the model's confidence scores were consistently high, because the cost of one bad autonomous refund at scale outweighed the latency savings of removing the gate. Meanwhile the FAQ-answering path never routed through that same approval flow at all -- only answers flagged by a separate grounding check got a review step, so most queries returned synchronously. Cost optimization only got real investment after both of those gates were already correctly placed -- before that, cost numbers were guessed, and optimizing a guessed number isn't a real trade-off decision at all.

weak_answer.framework: You can build it yourself, put the logic in each place that needs it, or just buy something. Depends what's easier.
weak_answer.applied_example: We usually just build things ourselves.

strong_answer.framework: The deciding question is whether there's a real cross-consumer question this capability needs to answer, not just whether multiple consumers happen to need similar logic. Embedding the logic separately in each consumer is fastest for the first one but gets structurally worse with each additional consumer -- N independently-evolving copies drift apart and can never answer a cross-consumer question without reconciling them after the fact. A standalone shared service costs more upfront (a new deployable, a real interface contract) but is the only option that can answer that cross-consumer question correctly. Buying only makes sense when the capability is genuinely commodity and doesn't need deep integration with your own domain model -- otherwise the 'vendor relationship' itself becomes the new coupling problem.
strong_answer.applied_example: Two platforms I worked on both needed real usage-cost metering. The first draft embedded corrected metering logic separately in each platform's own codebase. Partway through, it became clear that a real cross-platform budget total was structurally impossible that way -- two platforms each computing 'their own' cost can never produce one trustworthy total without a shared ledger underneath both. That single, initially-unstated requirement flipped the decision to a standalone metering service both platforms called into, matching how every other shared capability in that org was already its own single-purpose service. Buying a generic FinOps vendor tool was never seriously on the table, since it would have needed as much integration work to understand each platform's specific provider/pricing model as building it ourselves, while giving up control over how budget-breach enforcement connected to each platform's own kill-switch.

weak_answer.framework: You should either centralize governance or let each team do its own thing. I'd probably centralize it.
weak_answer.applied_example: One team handling it all seems simpler.

strong_answer.framework: This isn't a binary pick-a-side decision -- the real skill is drawing the line correctly within one system: centralize the decision surface, federate the implementation detail. What should centralize: the policy engine itself, the audit-trail format, the definition of what counts as high-risk, and the human-approval mechanism -- if every team invents its own version of 'does this need approval,' you get inconsistent risk tolerance and no way to answer 'what did any agent do last month' without querying N differently-shaped systems. What should stay federated: which specific actions a given team's agents are allowed to take, and the domain-specific logic for detecting risk in that team's own context, since a payments team's definition of high-risk (large refund amounts) is legitimately different from a content team's (PII exposure), and centralizing that specific judgment doesn't scale.
strong_answer.applied_example: In an org I worked with, orchestration (routing work to specialist agents, which changed constantly as new capabilities were added) and governance (policy decisions, human approval, signed audit trails, which needed to stay stable and auditable) were split into genuinely separate systems rather than one merged framework. The instinctive design -- one big system that both routes work and enforces policy -- looks simpler at first but breaks exactly where this framework predicts: either governance changes get rushed through at orchestration's fast iteration speed, which is unsafe, or orchestration changes get slowed by governance's necessarily slower review cadence, which is its own cost. Keeping them separate let each evolve at the pace its own risk profile actually required.

weak_answer.framework: You can train your own model, fine-tune an existing one, or just use an API. Training your own is probably the best option if you can afford it.
weak_answer.applied_example: Bigger companies usually train their own models.

strong_answer.framework: The right choice depends on what's actually being optimized for, not a default assumption that owning the model is inherently the more sophisticated answer. Training from scratch is only justified when the differentiation needed genuinely can't come from fine-tuning an existing model, or when owning the full model is itself the strategic asset -- a real position for a company whose business model is selling model access or safety research directly. Fine-tuning an open-weight model costs much less and gives real control over the specific behavior being tuned, but inherits the base model's capability ceiling and licensing terms -- this is the right call when the differentiation is in the tuning, not the base capability. Calling a vendor API (possibly more than one) is lowest upfront cost and fastest to ship, at the cost of inheriting that vendor's roadmap and pricing -- and a single vendor may not cover the full capability and cost surface a real product needs.
strong_answer.applied_example: Every service I've built that calls an LLM at all has used a vendor API rather than a model trained or fine-tuned in-house, because the differentiation that work actually needed to prove was in the governance, orchestration, and cost-metering layers around the model call, not in model capability itself -- training or fine-tuning a model would have required data and compute resources genuinely out of scope for what those layers needed to demonstrate. Separately, a serving-layer simulation project I worked on modeled real inference-serving mechanics (batching, memory management) without needing real trained weights at all, since the goal was proving serving-architecture understanding cheaply, a distinct scope decision from the model-strategy question but motivated by the same resource-constraint reasoning.
